title: What does 99.9% uptime mean for inference?
gist: Together AI lays out a framework mapping inference-uptime tiers (99%, 99.9%, 99.99%) to specific failure domains (node, data center, region) and architectural requirements, arguing that infrastructure ownership -- not the published SLA number -- determines whether a provider can actually deliver on it.

insights.claim: 99% inference uptime corresponds to surviving node-level failures (GPU hardware faults, driver crashes, thermal events) and requires automated health checking, node draining, and fast replica replacement within a single data center.
insights.speaker: Ryan Lucchese, Sarung Tripathi
insights.so_what: Investors evaluating GPU cloud/inference vendors can use this as a diligence rubric: a 99% SLA only insures against node failure, not facility-level outages.
insights.quote: 99% means your architecture can survive node-level failures: GPU hardware faults, driver crashes, thermal events.

insights.claim: 99.9% inference uptime requires model weights deployed across two facilities with enough capacity on each side to absorb full load and live traffic routed to both simultaneously, not a cold standby.
insights.speaker: Ryan Lucchese, Sarung Tripathi
insights.so_what: Most vendors' '99.9%' claims implicitly promise continuous dual-facility live routing; buyers should verify this architecturally rather than accept the number at face value.
insights.quote: The architectural decision that defines whether a provider actually delivers this tier is whether they run live traffic to both facilities continuously, or maintain a cold standby. We chose continuous.

insights.claim: 99.99% inference uptime requires multi-region deployment with availability-zone redundancy and reserved (not merely routable) failover capacity sized to absorb a full regional outage.
insights.speaker: Ryan Lucchese, Sarung Tripathi
insights.so_what: The four-nines tier implies idle standing capacity sitting unused in a failover region -- a real cost structure investors should factor into GPU-cloud unit economics.
insights.quote: The key word here is reserved. Not “we can route traffic there” but “we have room sitting idle there right now.”

insights.claim: Because GPU inference systems are tuned for extreme performance (up to 1M tokens/minute per GPU at 200 TPS, or sub-50ms time-to-first-token on voice models), each additional nine of reliability becomes exponentially harder to add without sacrificing performance headroom.
insights.speaker: Ryan Lucchese, Sarung Tripathi
insights.so_what: Reliability and raw throughput are in tension for GPU inference infra; vendors claiming both top-tier uptime and top-tier throughput should be scrutinized for which they actually optimize for.
insights.quote: Hitting 1M tokens per minute per GPU at 200 TPS, or sub-50ms TTFT on voice models with custom kernels, leaves limited slack. Adding reliability to a system like that is exponentially harder with each nine.

insights.claim: GPU inference providers that rent capacity from a hyperscaler or neocloud do not own their power/cooling/network failure domains, so when a fault occurs there they must file a support ticket to the underlying infrastructure owner and wait in queue, unlike a vertically integrated provider.
insights.speaker: Ryan Lucchese, Sarung Tripathi
insights.so_what: This is a direct competitive jab at GPU-cloud resellers/aggregators; investors comparing inference vendors should ask whether the vendor owns its data centers or is itself a tenant, since that gates real recovery speed.
insights.quote: A provider renting capacity from a hyperscaler or neocloud doesn’t own their failure domains. When something breaks at the power or cooling layer, they’re filing a ticket with whoever does.

insights.claim: Together AI markets 'chip-to-token' full-stack ownership across hardware, network, storage, and software as its differentiator, claiming a single support ticket resolves issues across all layers versus a multi-hop ticket chain at competitors.
insights.speaker: Ryan Lucchese, Sarung Tripathi
insights.so_what: Positions Together AI's vertical integration as a defensible moat against thinner GPU-cloud middlemen, relevant to competitive assessment of the inference-hosting market.
insights.quote: When you’re with Together AI, one ticket covers the hardware, network, storage, and software because we have chip-to-token visibility across our entire global footprint.

insights.claim: Together AI names Cursor, Decagon, Cartesia, and Yutori as production customers whose products depend on its inference uptime.
insights.speaker: Ryan Lucchese, Sarung Tripathi
insights.so_what: Confirms Together AI's customer base spans coding assistants, voice AI, and agent products -- useful reference set for assessing which downstream AI companies are exposed to Together AI's infrastructure risk.
insights.quote: Together runs inference for teams like Cursor, Decagon, Cartesia, and Yutori.

insights.claim: ECC errors in GPU VRAM are the most common hardware failure Together AI observes, and are especially dangerous because they silently corrupt model weights while requests still return successfully, making outputs untrustworthy without any visible error.
insights.speaker: Ryan Lucchese, Sarung Tripathi
insights.so_what: Silent data corruption is a materially worse failure mode than outright downtime for AI product reliability -- investors in AI-dependent products should ask vendors specifically how they detect silent output-quality degradation, not just uptime.
insights.quote: ECC errors in VRAM (the most common thing we see; they corrupt weights silently, so requests return but outputs aren't trustworthy)

insights.claim: Active health checks (synthetic load tests) catch failure modes that only appear under real GPU load but require dedicating spare capacity to run, creating a direct tradeoff against running fleets near 100% utilization for efficiency.
insights.speaker: Ryan Lucchese, Sarung Tripathi
insights.so_what: Reveals a structural cost embedded in high-reliability GPU inference: utilization and reliability trade off against each other, which should show up in gross margin differences between reliability tiers.
insights.quote: You can’t exercise a GPU that’s already serving traffic, since this creates obvious tension. Everyone wants to run near 100% utilization, and maintaining excess capacity for health checks just trades reliability for inefficiency.

insights.claim: Failure modes cascade across infrastructure layers rather than staying isolated: a storage outage can surface as a capacity problem, and a thermal event can degrade output quality long before any health alert fires.
insights.speaker: Ryan Lucchese, Sarung Tripathi
insights.so_what: Suggests that single-layer monitoring (e.g., just GPU health) is insufficient for AI infra reliability; cross-layer correlation capability is a differentiator worth probing in vendor due diligence.
insights.quote: A storage hiccup surfaces as a capacity problem, a thermal event shows up as degraded output quality long before any health alert fires.

insights.claim: A single-data-center deployment is capped at roughly 99% uptime regardless of in-rack redundancy because building-level events (thermal issues, substation failures, edge router failures) take the whole site down, and untested backup systems often fail when actually invoked.
insights.speaker: Ryan Lucchese, Sarung Tripathi
insights.so_what: Investors should treat any single-DC inference deployment as inherently capped near two-nines availability, independent of the vendor's marketing claims.
insights.quote: A redundant system that isn’t regularly tested under real conditions is like calling a player off the bench who hasn’t practiced in six weeks. The failover might be there on paper, whether it works is a different question.

themes.theme: SLA measurement methodology and buyer diligence

insights.claim: Together AI reports delivered uptime of 99.9%, contractual SLAs of 99% for single-DC and 99.9% for multi-DC deployments, failover time measured in seconds, and request volume above 2 billion tokens per minute, with uptime measured at inference completion rather than at the load balancer.
insights.speaker: Ryan Lucchese, Sarung Tripathi
insights.so_what: Gives investors a concrete, checkable baseline (2B+ TPM, 99.9% delivered) against which to benchmark other inference vendors' disclosed metrics.
insights.quote: We measure at inference completion, not at the gateway. A request that reaches the load balancer but fails at the GPU is downtime in our accounting.

insights.claim: Availability and performance are separate contractual guarantees in Provisioned Throughput deployments; a service that is technically 'up' but delivering only 30% of contracted tokens-per-second is not meeting its obligation even though it would count as uptime under naive measurement.
insights.speaker: Ryan Lucchese, Sarung Tripathi
insights.so_what: Investors modeling GPU-inference vendor risk should treat throughput degradation as a distinct failure mode from downtime, since headline uptime numbers can mask severe performance shortfalls.
insights.quote: A service that’s up but delivering 30% of contracted throughput isn’t meeting the deal.

insights.claim: Together AI proposes a specific due-diligence checklist for buyers of inference infrastructure covering infrastructure ownership, full-stack hardware expertise, capacity/failover testing practices, and SLA measurement methodology (e.g., whether client-side retries count against uptime).
insights.speaker: Ryan Lucchese, Sarung Tripathi
insights.so_what: Provides a ready-made vendor-evaluation checklist investors or portfolio companies can apply when assessing any GPU inference provider, not just Together AI.
insights.quote: Is your SLA measured at the load balancer or at successful inference completion? Do client-side retries count against your uptime measurement?
insights.locator: What to ask before you commit to a provider
